input_count_context: Over 11300 participants; over 64600 responses to 26 questions
input_summary: ISED says the 30-day consultation received input from more than 11300 respondents and was the largest public consultation in ISED history.
final_strategy_response: The final strategy cites broad engagement but does not expose granular public response data inside the strategy itself.
app_use: How this was made; credibility context
gap_to_track: Need link to Open Government dataset and task-force reports when imported.

input_count_context: Official summary says Task Force members delivered 32 reports; Annex B lists members by focus area
input_summary: Minister Solomon named an AI Strategy Task Force from academia, industry, think tanks, NGOs, government-adjacent and other expert communities.
final_strategy_response: The final strategy reflects many task-force themes, especially adoption, sovereign infrastructure, scaling champions, and trust.
gap_to_track: Confirm exact member/report count from Open Government package before public copy.

input_count_context: Public responses and Task Force reports analyzed with SimpleSurvey plus LLM classification pipeline
input_theme: AI used to analyze AI consultation
input_summary: ISED used NLP and LLMs including Cohere, OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google models, with human validation and external methodology feedback.
source_section: Use of AI in the engagement analysis
final_strategy_response: The final strategy does not make the methodology a central public-facing accountability feature.
gap_to_track: Track whether dataset, prompts, taxonomy, validation process are available.

input_summary: Respondents called for talent attraction, retention, scholarships, immigration reforms, academia-industry-government links, and responsible governance.
final_strategy_response: The strategy answers with literacy, training, youth placements, institutes, and talent supports, but less detail on immigration and compensation.
gap_to_track: Separate advanced AI talent from broad public literacy.

consultation_focus_area: AI adoption across industry and governments
input_summary: Respondents wanted real-world adoption in health care, agriculture, public services, productivity, and public education, with warnings about premature deployment, privacy, environmental harm, and job displacement.
source_section: Online consultation - AI adoption across industry and governments
primary_personas: Small Business Owner; Workplace AI Adopter; Future Canadian
final_strategy_response: The strategy answers strongly on adoption programs and missions, but safeguards and sector standards remain less concrete.
gap_to_track: Track sector-specific standards and measurement details.

input_theme: Keep IP and companies in Canada
input_summary: Respondents wanted grants, tax credits, investor incentives, public funding, Canadian ownership of IP/data, and protections for creative industries.
pillars: Scaling Canadian champions; Sovereign AI foundation; Empowering Canadians
final_strategy_response: The strategy answers with commercialization, IP support, capital, procurement and compute, but creator compensation/copyright is still open.

input_summary: Respondents wanted tailored growth supports, mentorship, government procurement, capital, domestic market creation, and caution around AI bubbles.
source_section: Online consultation - Scaling champions and attracting investments
final_strategy_response: The strategy strongly reflects this with champion-scaling, government as anchor customer, compute and capital, but implementation criteria remain open.
gap_to_track: Track who qualifies, what public gets back, and procurement rules.

consultation_focus_area: Safe AI systems and public trust
input_summary: Respondents called for risk-based certification, independent audits, clear AI-use disclosure, oversight bodies, penalties for non-compliance, public education, and AI literacy.
source_section: Online consultation - Safe AI systems and public trust
final_strategy_response: The strategy names trust, safety institute, transparency, watermarking and future laws, but enforceable obligations are mostly pending.
gap_to_track: Track hard-law follow-through and enforcement authority.

input_summary: Respondents wanted K-12 and post-secondary AI literacy, stackable credentials, lifelong learning, workplace training, critical thinking, ethics, and equitable access.
primary_personas: Student / Future Worker; Workplace AI Adopter
final_strategy_response: The strategy answers with AI literacy, learning kits, educator support, student agents, and worker training, but curriculum execution depends on institutions and provinces.

input_summary: Respondents wanted domestic compute, shared data resources, connectivity, Canadian-controlled infrastructure, and mitigation of energy and water impacts.
primary_personas: Future Canadian; AI Entrepreneur; Concerned Canadian
final_strategy_response: The strategy strongly answers compute and sovereignty, but local energy, water, permitting, and community benefit details remain open.
gap_to_track: Track environmental standards and public-benefit rules.

input_summary: Respondents wanted AI security centres, red-team exercises, data sovereignty, supply-chain security, strict liability, human-in-the-loop systems, and critical infrastructure protection.
pillars: Protecting Canadians and safeguarding democracy; Sovereign AI foundation
final_strategy_response: The strategy addresses cybersecurity, fraud defence, safety institute, and sovereign infrastructure, but liability and incident obligations need detail.

input_summary: Respondents raised unauthorized use of creative works, compensation, opt-in consent, Canadian data ownership, marginalized-group underrepresentation, audits, equity funding, and Indigenous/community concerns.
primary_personas: Future Canadian; Canadian Creator; Concerned Canadian
pillars: Empowering Canadians; Protecting Canadians; Trusted partnerships
final_strategy_response: The strategy includes culture, accessibility, Indigenous-led AI, and creative technology funding, but copyright, compensation, and Indigenous data sovereignty mechanisms remain open.
gap_to_track: Track specific copyright and Indigenous data governance instruments.

consultation_focus_area: AI adoption across industry and governments
input_summary: Task Force reports called for streamlined procurement, standardized playbooks, pilot-to-scale frameworks, ROI metrics, goal tracking, and SME/underserved-sector support.
source_section: Task Force reports - AI adoption across industry and governments
primary_personas: Small Business Owner; Workplace AI Adopter
final_strategy_response: The strategy answers with adoption programs and government anchor adoption but has less visible practical playbook detail than experts requested.
gap_to_track: Use Singapore comparator for practical playbook design.

input_summary: Task Force reports emphasized commercialization pathways, tax reforms, SR&ED/IRAP changes, vouchers, incubators, and retaining Canadian IP.
source_section: Task Force reports - Commercialization of AI
final_strategy_response: The strategy answers the general need through capital, commercialization, procurement and IP programs, but specific tax/SR&ED/IRAP reforms are not the main visible mechanism.
gap_to_track: Track whether program reforms appear in budgets or implementation plans.

consultation_focus_area: Safe AI systems and public trust
input_summary: Task Force reports called for transparent dashboards, trust labels, independent auditing platforms, tiered risk assessments, privacy and cybersecurity standards, and public participation.
source_section: Task Force reports - Safe AI systems and public trust
final_strategy_response: The strategy mentions certification, standards, transparency, watermarking, and safety institute activity, but public dashboards and audit requirements remain to be specified.
gap_to_track: Track if certification becomes voluntary or mandatory.

input_summary: Task Force reports called for domestic data centres, high-performance computing clusters, data campuses, AI corridors, shared research platforms, and equitable access.
final_strategy_response: The strategy strongly answers with public supercomputer and sovereign infrastructure, but equitable access and public-benefit rules need detail.
gap_to_track: Track access rules for SMEs, researchers, public-interest users.

input_summary: Business submissions emphasized sovereign infrastructure, talent pipelines, targeted incentives, unified data platforms, consortiums, immigration, sector initiatives, procurement, Canadian-owned SMEs, and IP retention.
pillars: Powering AI adoption; Scaling Canadian champions; Sovereign AI foundation
final_strategy_response: The strategy reflects many business asks, especially infrastructure, adoption, scale-up, capital, and procurement.
gap_to_track: Track whether SME-specific access avoids capture by larger firms.

input_summary: Government submissions wanted national research networks, secure compute, public-official training, human rights assessments, standardized procurement, transparency, interoperability, and oversight.
final_strategy_response: The strategy reflects public-service adoption and trust themes, but human-rights assessment, registry, appeal, and procurement details are still implementation questions.
gap_to_track: Could become a dedicated public-sector subtopic.

input_summary: NGO submissions emphasized union, Indigenous, intersectional feminist, public oversight, marginalized groups, copyright, Indigenous innovation, community-driven oversight, and equitable access.
primary_personas: Concerned Canadian; Future Canadian; Canadian Creator
pillars: Protecting Canadians; Empowering Canadians; Trusted partnerships
final_strategy_response: The strategy reflects inclusion, culture, Indigenous-led AI and trust language, but hard oversight, copyright and community governance remain open.
gap_to_track: Track which NGO asks were not acknowledged directly in the final strategy.
